stem: In building the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = [[c]]e^{[[k]]x} \), one needs the \( n \)th derivative at \( 0 \). Compute \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: Each derivative of \( e^{[[k]]x} \) picks up a factor of \( [[k]] \), so \( f^{(n)}(x) = [[c]]\cdot[[k]]^{n}e^{[[k]]x} \), and at \( x=0 \), \( f^{([[n]])}(0) = [[c]]\cdot[[k]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the exponent entirely, keeping only the first derivative's factor of \( [[k]] \).
This multiplies by \( n \) instead of raising \( [[k]] \) to the \( n \)th power.
This is off by one power of \( [[k]] \), as if the derivative order were \( n-1 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: The function \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1-[[k]]x} \) has Maclaurin series \( \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} [[k]]^{i}x^{i} \). What is the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) in this series?
solution: Since \( \dfrac{1}{1-[[k]]x} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} ([[k]]x)^{i} = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} [[k]]^{i}x^{i} \), the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) is \( [[k]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( k \) and \( n \) together instead of raising \( k \) to the \( n \)th power.
This applies a power-rule-style derivative factor \( n \cdot k^{n-1} \), confusing this with a derivative coefficient.
This is off by one power of \( [[k]] \), as if the coefficient of \( x^{n-1} \) were being asked for.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: The \( n \)th-degree Maclaurin polynomial for \( f(x)=e^{x} \) is \( P_{n}(x) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} \dfrac{x^{i}}{i!} \). Compute \( P_{[[n]]}([[x0]]) \).
solution: \( P_{[[n]]}([[x0]]) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{[[n]]} \dfrac{([[x0]])^{i}}{i!} = [[answer]] \), a finite partial sum, not the exact value \( e^{[[x0]]} \) of the full infinite series.
distractor_rationales: This reports the exact value \( e^{x_0} \) of the infinite series, not the finite \( n \)th-degree partial sum.
This keeps only the LAST term of the partial sum, dropping every earlier term.
This drops the last term of the partial sum, stopping the sum one term too early.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: For \( f(x)=\sin x \), every derivative is bounded by \( 1 \) in absolute value, so the Lagrange remainder after the degree-\( [[n]] \) Taylor polynomial about \( 0 \), evaluated at \( x=[[x0]] \), satisfies \( |R_{[[n]]}([[x0]])| \le \dfrac{|[[x0]]|^{[[n]]+1}}{([[n]]+1)!} \). Compute this Lagrange error bound.
solution: The Lagrange remainder bound is \( \dfrac{|x|^{n+1}}{(n+1)!} \) here (since the \( (n+1) \)th derivative of \( \sin \) or \( \cos \) is bounded by \( 1 \)). Substituting \( x=[[x0]] \) and \( n=[[n]] \) gives \( \dfrac{[[x0]]^{[[n]]+1}}{([[n]]+1)!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong degree throughout, computing the bound for a degree-\( (n-1) \) polynomial instead of degree \( n \).
This uses the correct power of \( x \) but the wrong factorial (\( n! \) instead of \( (n+1)! \)).
This uses the correct factorial but the wrong power of \( x \) (\( x^{n} \) instead of \( x^{n+1} \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: The Maclaurin series for \( f(x)=\ln(1+x) \) is \( \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{i+1}x^{i}}{i} \). Compute the degree-\( [[n]] \) partial sum \( P_{[[n]]}([[x0]]) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} \dfrac{(-1)^{i+1}([[x0]])^{i}}{i} \).
solution: \( P_{[[n]]}([[x0]]) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{[[n]]} \dfrac{(-1)^{i+1}([[x0]])^{i}}{i} = [[answer]] \), the finite alternating partial sum, not the exact value \( \ln(1+[[x0]]) \) of the full series (which may not even converge here if \( |x_0|>1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This reports the exact value \( \ln(1+x_0) \) of the infinite series, not the finite partial sum (and the series may not even converge for this \( x_0 \)).
This keeps only the last term of the partial sum, dropping every earlier term.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
